subject: Fix issues in existing entity classes
commit_message:
Fix issues in existing entity classes

Change-Id: I862a5c413f5cf4d0e472a527cd6dc5a32c28459c
Issue-ID: POLICY-3871
Signed-off-by: a.sreekumar <ajith.sreekumar@bell.ca>

entries.message:
Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Build Successful 

https://jenkins.onap.org/job/policy-docker-master-verify-java/339/ : SUCCESS

Logs: https://logs.onap.org/production/vex-yul-ecomp-jenkins-1/policy-docker-master-verify-java/339

Patch Set 1:

This change is ready for review.

Patch Set 1:

Thanks Ajith, I think you are the first one with this review doing actual upgrade/downgrade here.   I have a question about the actual data contents, let's say that you have the 2 new columns added in jakarta in jpatoscapolicy_target with data, and now for whatever reason you go to istanbul, the columns are dropped and the data goes away.    Should the data from those columns go somewhere else to maintain the integrity of the actual data for the istanbul layout.    May be the example is not that good but I think you get what I trying to say, we are modifying the layouts of the tables properly, but the question is if we are missing redistribution of the actual data contents (and not lose them)?    The question go in both directions upgrade and downgrade.

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> Thanks Ajith, I think you are the first one with this review doing actual upgrade/downgrade here.   I have a question about the actual data contents, let's say that you have the 2 new columns added in jakarta in jpatoscapolicy_target with data, and now for whatever reason you go to istanbul, the columns are dropped and the data goes away.    Should the data from those columns go somewhere else to maintain the integrity of the actual data for the istanbul layout.    May be the example is not that good but I think you get what I trying to say, we are modifying the layouts of the tables properly, but the question is if we are missing redistribution of the actual data contents (and not lose them)?    The question go in both directions upgrade and downgrade.

With the current implementation of the db-migrator, I think we don't give any consideration to the data in any of the tables. So, what happens today is,
say for e.g., there is 1 row in jpatoscapolicy_target "name1", "value1" under columns name, value.
When upgrading from I to J, two new columns are added: toscaPolicyName and toscaPolicyVersion. In the already existing rows, the 2 new columns will have empty values.
Similary on downgrading from J to I, any data  under columns toscaPolicyName and toscaPolicyVersion will be lost, and only the data under name and version will remain.

The tables affected in this review are not getting used really today, but I think your question is valid. If we want to save this data that is getting removed, then I think the question is how and where to save this data! I think "backup and restore" is the first thing we should have. Also, if we want to redistribute the data to a different table, maybe we could have scripts for that depending on how we want to do it.

Patch Set 1:

I have a question about the actual data contents, let's say that you have the 2 new columns added in jakarta in jpatoscapolicy_target with data, and now for whatever reason you go to istanbul, the columns are dropped and the data goes away.    Should the data from those columns go somewhere else to maintain the integrity of the actual data for the istanbul layout.    May be the example is not that good but I think you get what I trying to say, we are modifying the layouts of the tables properly, but the question is if we are missing redistribution of the actual data contents (and not lose them)?    The question go in both directions upgrade and downgrade.
> 
> With the current implementation of the db-migrator, I think we don't give any consideration to the data in any of the tables. So, what happens today is,
> say for e.g., there is 1 row in jpatoscapolicy_target "name1", "value1" under columns name, value.
> When upgrading from I to J, two new columns are added: toscaPolicyName and toscaPolicyVersion. In the already existing rows, the 2 new columns will have empty values.
> Similary on downgrading from J to I, any data  under columns toscaPolicyName and toscaPolicyVersion will be lost, and only the data under name and version will remain.
> 
> The tables affected in this review are not getting used really today, but I think your question is valid. If we want to save this data that is getting removed, then I think the question is how and where to save this data! I think "backup and restore" is the first thing we should have. Also, if we want to redistribute the data to a different table, maybe we could have scripts for that depending on how we want to do it.

Similar thoughts as both of you, but I am not sure if we really want to store data separately for upgrades/downgrades. I mean if someone decides to move ahead and due to that if few alterations are made to db, ex - columns getting added or deleted. And later changed mind to rollback the version. Maintaining the data integrity will be tricky and we may have to create an interim schema to store the old data.
Best option in this case seems to be backup/restore for now. We can suggest to take a backup before making any changes. Infact, keeping regular backups is even more safe.

For this review (as Ajith said), it is safe as those tables were not actually used.

If we want to save this data that is getting removed, then I think the question is how and where to save this data! I think "backup and restore" is the first thing we should have. If we want to save this data that is getting removed, then I think the question is how and where to save this data! I think "backup and restore" is the first thing we should have. Also, if we want to redistribute the data to a different table, maybe we could have scripts for that depending on how we want to do it.
> > 
> > Similar thoughts as both of you, but I am not sure if we really want to store data separately for upgrades/downgrades. I mean if someone decides to move ahead and due to that if few alterations are made to db, ex - columns getting added or deleted. And later changed mind to rollback the version. Maintaining the data integrity will be tricky and we may have to create an interim schema to store the old data.
> > Best option in this case seems to be backup/restore for now. We can suggest to take a backup before making any changes. Infact, keeping regular backups is even more safe.
> > 
> > For this review (as Ajith said), it is safe as those tables were not actually used.

> Yes, agreed.   I just wanted to identify situations when the data migration may be problematic, we may have a little bit of a hole on that area for some situations.

Doing rollback on an upgrade where there was extra stuff added is always tricky. I guess if the rollback happens before any of the new tables are really used it should be OK but if it's after some time when the new tables are used then it could be very tricky.....but maybe that situation doesn't happen much....rollback probably happens soon after upgrade or not at all.
Change has been successfully merged by Liam Fallon
